lesson.menu_desc: Reading a board brief + formal connectors -- 28 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 7 -- The Board Presentation -- Executive Communication
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Rafael Pelizaro | Aula 7 -- The Board Presentation

paras: Next Thursday I present the Phoenix platform to the board. The board is a tough audience: they have ten minutes, they read every slide, and they want the key point first. So here is a short brief. I open with a one-paragraph overview, I keep the agenda to three items, and I save the detail for the questions. The goal is simple: leave the directors confident that the project is in safe hands.

Here is the overview. Phoenix replaces four old systems with one cloud platform, and it is now ninety percent built. Let me share two highlights. First, the pilot cut the average login time from eight seconds to two. In addition, the running cost is fifteen percent below the old budget. However, I will be honest about one issue: the security review took longer than planned. As a result, the full go-live moves from March to early April.

To summarize, the message for the board is short and clear. The platform is faster, it is cheaper, and the one-month delay is under control. My recommendation is to approve the April go-live and release the final budget now. I will close the agenda with that single key point, and I will keep my tone calm and formal, because a confident summary is what an executive audience remembers.
reading.source: Adapted for class use from an internal IT board-presentation brief

gist.prompt: What is the best one-line summary of this board brief?
choices: The Phoenix project has failed the security review and the board has cancelled it.
Phoenix is faster and cheaper, and Rafael recommends approving an April go-live despite a one-month delay.
The board wants a long, detailed report with every technical slide explained.

items: Rafael plans to open the presentation with the smallest detail and save the key point for the end.
He opens with the key point and the overview first, and saves detail for the questions.
The pilot has reduced the average login time from eight seconds to two.
He lists it as the first highlight in the overview.
The security review has pushed the full go-live from March to early April.
He is honest that the review took longer, so go-live moves by about one month.
Rafael's recommendation is to cancel the project until the budget is clearer.
His recommendation is to approve the April go-live and release the final budget now.
Rafael puts the key point first and the detail last. When you present to senior people, do you do the same? Why or why not?
He is honest about one issue instead of hiding it. How does an audience usually react when you admit a problem clearly?
If you had ten minutes with your board, which single recommendation about your project would you want them to remember?

listenings.text: Hi, this is Rafael. I am recording a quick rehearsal of my Phoenix board presentation for Thursday, so I can hear how it sounds. Let me start with the overview. Phoenix replaces four old systems with one cloud platform, and it is now ninety percent built. The board is a busy audience, so I will keep the agenda to three items and put the key point first. Now the highlights. First, the pilot cut the average login time from eight seconds to two. In addition, the running cost is fifteen percent below the old budget. However, I want to be honest about one issue. The security review took longer than planned. As a result, the full go-live moves from March to early April. To summarize, the platform is faster, it is cheaper, and the one-month delay is under control. My recommendation is simple: approve the April go-live and release the final budget now. I will close with that single key point. I will keep my tone calm and formal, I will use connectors like however, in addition, and as a result to link my ideas, and I will leave the board feeling confident that the project is in safe hands.

Hi Rafael, this is Diana from the board, leaving you a short voicemail after your dry run. Overall, the brief is strong, and I want to give you three quick notes before Thursday. First, your overview is excellent. You put the key point first and you kept it to one paragraph, which is exactly what a busy board needs. Second, I liked that you were honest about the security delay instead of hiding it. When you say as a result the go-live moves to April, please add one sentence on how you are keeping that month under control, because the directors will ask. Third, your recommendation is clear, but say it twice: once at the start and once in your summary, so it is the last thing we hear. One small thing on delivery. On your second slide you speak very fast. Slow down, take a breath after each highlight, and let the numbers land. You sound most confident when you pause. Send me the final agenda the night before, and I will make sure you get your full ten minutes. Good work, Rafael, this is nearly there.
